1.matchText: Reconstruction is the subject of the narrative that comes right before this one
1.blurb: The Reconstruction narrative is the story right before this one in the chain, and it ends here. After a decade of trying to remake the South into a multiracial democracy, a disputed 1876 presidential election was settled by the Compromise of 1877, the last federal troops were withdrawn, and the experiment was abandoned. Everything Jim Crow does in the Roaring Twenties starts from that bargain.

1.matchText: Ku Klux Klan, then the rifle clubs
1.blurb: The first Klan was not a fringe but an insurgency. The Reconstruction narrative's chapter on the Klan Wars shows it systematically murdering Black voters and officeholders, and then shows the federal government briefly crushing it with the Enforcement Acts, proof that it could have been stopped. The second Klan of the 1920s, the subject of Chapter 10 here, was a different animal grown from the same root.

Sharecropping was not inevitable; it was what replaced the broken promise of land. The Reconstruction narrative's chapter on 'Forty Acres, and What Replaced It' walks through how land redistribution failed and how the crop-lien system trapped freedpeople in perpetual debt. That trap is still closed sixty years later, which is why the Great Migration of Chapter 13 happens at all.

The 1896 Plessy v. Ferguson ruling made 'separate but equal' the law of the land. The Reconstruction narrative's final chapter, on the long afterlife of the era, traces how the Supreme Court disarmed the Fourteenth Amendment and how the Lost Cause and the Dunning School rewrote the whole story, the legal and cultural machinery the twenties inherited intact.

3.blurb: The white mob violence of 1919 was not random fury; it ran the same instrument that overthrew Reconstruction. The Reconstruction narrative's 'Redemption' chapter documents the template, deputized white paramilitaries, the destruction of prosperous Black institutions, no prosecutions, and the record actively suppressed, and the Red Summer follows it almost step for step.

3.matchText: 1919 was also one of the worst years of anti-Black violence in American history
3.blurb: A crucial difference from earlier waves was that many of the Black men in these cities were veterans who had been to France, were armed, and fought back. The Civil Rights narrative opens on exactly this thread, returning Black veterans, the long NAACP legal campaign, the slow runway to Brown, the tradition of organized self-defense and mass action that the institutions built in this decade would launch.

5.matchText: the migration is the failure of Reconstruction's land promise, sixty years deferred, finally cashing out
5.blurb: The migration is one story with Reconstruction, not two. The Reconstruction narrative's 'Slavery by Another Name' chapter sets out the 'forty acres' promise and its swift reversal, the Black Codes, and the birth of sharecropping. When emancipation came without land, the freed people were forced into a debt trap with no way out but, sixty years later, the road north.

9.blurb: The text names the ancestor outright. The Antebellum America narrative's Market Revolution chapter covers the Know-Nothings: a mass anti-Catholic, anti-immigrant secret order with rituals, lodges, and real electoral power that rose during the Irish and German wave and collapsed when the slavery crisis pulled the floor out. The pattern recurs almost exactly seventy years later.

10.matchText: its murder campaign against Black voters and officeholders helped overthrow the multiracial democracy
10.blurb: The first Klan did not fade; it won. The Reconstruction narrative's 'Klan Wars' chapter shows its terror campaign against Black voters and officeholders helping to overthrow the multiracial democracy of Reconstruction, after which it could stand down because its work was done. That is the crucial distinction this chapter builds on.

10.matchText: the Lost Cause and the academic Dunning School had already installed in textbooks
10.blurb: The Birth of a Nation did not invent its flattering story; it dramatized the version the Lost Cause and the Dunning School had already made respectable. The Reconstruction narrative's final chapter, on the era's long afterlife, dismantles exactly that version, the sanitized history the twenties inherited and the second Klan was sold on.

10.matchText: running in an unbroken line from the 1870s Redemption paramilitaries
10.blurb: The 1924 convention vote is one data point on a fifty-year curve. The Reconstruction narrative's 'Mississippi Plan' chapter shows the same party's complicity with organized white-supremacist violence in its perfected form, the rifle clubs, the massacres, a state lost on purpose, the unbroken line this paragraph points at running forward to 1924 and beyond.

10.matchText: The Reconstruction and Civil Rights narratives show the same party's complicity
10.blurb: The line runs forward as well as back. The Civil Rights narrative's final chapter, on the long arc back to 1877, closes the fifty-year curve this paragraph names, tracing the same party's relationship to organized white-supremacist violence from Redemption through the 1920s and into the 1950s and 60s.

13.matchText: the migration is that sixty-year-old failure finally forcing people onto the road
13.blurb: The Great Migration is the unpaid bill of Reconstruction coming due. The Reconstruction narrative's 'Forty Acres, and What Replaced It' chapter explains why land redistribution failed and how the crop-lien trap held for generations. With no land and no capital to cushion them, Black Southerners were finally forced onto the road north in this decade.

13.matchText: the same Senate machinery being used to block federal protection of Black citizens since the 1870s
13.blurb: The 1922 anti-lynching filibuster was the continuation of a fifty-year system, not a fresh failure. The Reconstruction narrative's chapter on the Supreme Court disarming the Constitution shows the same federal machinery being turned against the protection of Black citizens from the 1870s on, the long pattern the NAACP's defeat sat inside.

13.matchText: the Civil Rights Era narrative identifies this same Randolph, sixteen years later
13.blurb: Mark the name and the year. The Civil Rights narrative's opening chapter identifies this same A. Philip Randolph as the man whose threatened 1941 march on Washington forced the first federal order against employment discrimination, the proof that organized mass action could extract concessions from Washington that became the later movement's strategic core. The pipeline runs straight from the 1925 porters' union to that.

13.matchText: that base is precisely what the movement two narratives down this chain would be launched from
13.blurb: The justice did not come in this decade; the base for it did. The Civil Rights narrative, two narratives down the chain, is launched from exactly what the twenties built, the porters' union and the NAACP, the migrant cities and the money and the audiences, and the confidence of a generation that had made the century's defining American music while shut out of the room.

15.blurb: The 1929 Crash was the fourth run of a known failure, not a bolt from the blue. The Antebellum America narrative walks through the Panic of 1837 in its 'King Andrew I' chapter, the same arc of boom, easy credit, collapse, and a banking class insisting all the way down that the fundamentals were sound.

15.matchText: the Early American Republic and Antebellum America narratives walk through
15.blurb: The pattern is older still. The Early American Republic narrative's 'Era of Good Feelings' chapter covers the Panic of 1819, the young country's first great speculative crash, the same structure of boom and easy credit and collapse that 1929 would run for the fourth time.

15.matchText: returning Black veterans, the heirs of the migration and the institutions Chapter 13 described
15.blurb: This is the bridge to the next narrative in the chain. The Civil Rights narrative opens on the Depression, the New Deal's mixed record, the second Great Migration, and the returning Black veterans of World War II, the heirs of the institutions Chapter 13 built, who carried the long campaign that became the movement.
